page: Amazon Web Services c7gd.8xlarge is a compute-optimized instance powered by 32 dedicated physical cores of the ARM64 AWS Neoverse-V1 processor running at 2.6 GHz. It features 64.0 GB of system memory, providing a 2.0 GB per core ratio. The instance is equipped with 1900 GB of local NVMe SSD instance storage and operates on the AWS Nitro hypervisor with a baseline network bandwidth of 15 Gbps.

Benchmark data indicates strong memory subsystem performance, with the Passmark memory score reaching the top-tier category and large-block read/write memory bandwidth classified as strong. Multi-core performance is also strong, as demonstrated by Geekbench multi-core and Passmark database operations scores. However, the architecture shows limitations in large-scale machine learning workloads, with prompt processing for a 70B LLM model registering in the poor performance tier.

Given its ARM64 architecture and dedicated physical cores, the instance offers high resource density and power efficiency relative to its compute class. The inclusion of 1900 GB of high-speed local NVMe SSD storage makes it highly suitable for applications requiring low-latency scratch space or high-speed caching. Recommended workloads include static web serving, database operations, multi-threaded data processing, and general compute-intensive tasks that benefit from local NVMe storage.
description: Amazon Web Services c7gd.8xlarge is a compute-optimized ARM64 instance powered by 32 dedicated AWS Neoverse-V1 physical cores running at 2.6 GHz. It features 64.0 GB of memory, a 1900 GB local NVMe SSD instance store, and a baseline network bandwidth of 15 Gbps. Operating on the Nitro hypervisor, this instance delivers strong multi-core and memory performance, with its Passmark memory score reaching the top-tier category and large-block memory bandwidth rated as strong. While it exhibits lower performance on large-scale LLM prompt processing, its high-speed local storage and strong database operations benchmark make it highly efficient for database workloads, static web serving, and multi-threaded data processing.
